title: Dry sow management in late pregnancy
subtitle: Feeding and condition management in the month before farrowing.
excerpt: The four weeks before farrowing set up the litter's birth weight, the sow's milk supply, and her stamina in labour. Underfeeding in this period reduces piglet birth weight and compromises colostrum quality. Overfeeding produces a fat sow who farrows slowly and is prone to savaging piglets. Getting body condition right in this window matters more than any other single feeding decision.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Pork, Sow nutrition and body condition guidance. https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Pigs (2003). https://www.gov.uk
Nadis, Dry sow management and nutrition. https://www.nadis.org.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: A pregnant sow that is not lactating. The dry period runs from weaning (or service in a gilt) to farrowing. The final four weeks of the dry period are the most critical for nutrition management, as this is when foetal growth is fastest and mammary gland development for milk production occurs.

A numerical assessment of a sow's fat reserves, scored on a 1 to 5 scale: 1 is severely thin, 5 is obese. The target at farrowing is BCS 3 to 3.5. A sow above BCS 4 is at increased risk of farrowing difficulties and savaging; a sow below BCS 2.5 will have reduced milk output and poor return to service after weaning.

The practice of increasing a sow's feed ration in the final two to three weeks of pregnancy to meet the rapid growth demands of the foetuses and to build energy reserves for farrowing and early lactation. Feed is typically increased from a maintenance ration of around 2.5 kg per day to 3 to 3.5 kg per day.

A female pig that has not yet had a litter. Gilts have higher nutritional requirements than mature sows at the same stage of pregnancy because they are still growing themselves. A gilt carrying her first litter should be fed slightly more generously than the table rates for mature sows.

content.text: needs careful feeding from service through to farrowing. Overfeeding in early and mid-pregnancy creates a fat sow and large foetuses that are harder to deliver. The strategic increase happens only in the last few weeks.
Score body condition every two weeks during pregnancy. Place your hands on the sow's back and run them firmly over the spine and hips. At

3 (the target), the backbone is felt with firm pressure but not seen, and the hip bones are palpable but not prominent. At BCS 2, the spine is visible. At BCS 4, the hip bones are hard to feel even with firm pressure and there is a visible crease along the back.

For the first 12 weeks of a 16-week pregnancy, feed to maintain condition. For most sows of 200 to 250 kg, this is 2 to 2.5 kg of dry sow pellets per day. Adjust for individual condition: a thin sow needs more, a fat sow needs less.

. Increase the ration gradually to 3 to 3.5 kg per day by week 14. Do not increase sharply in the final week: a sudden large feed increase in the last five days before farrowing can contribute to constipation and reduce feed intake during early lactation. The increase should be gradual, not stepped.

carrying her first litter is still growing herself. Her daily ration during pregnancy should be 5 to 10% higher than the equivalent mature sow at the same BCS. Monitor her weight closely: a gilt that loses condition in pregnancy will have a poor milk supply and a slow return to service.

A dry sow ration is different from a grower or lactation ration. It is lower in energy and higher in fibre. The fibre supports gut health and reduces stereotypic rooting behaviour in confined sows. If feeding sow-specific pellets is not practical, adding a source of high-fibre roughage (sugar beet pulp, unmolassed) alongside a grower ration partially compensates.

attrs.title: Reducing feed in the last 48 hours
attrs.body: Many producers reduce the feed ration by a third in the 24 to 48 hours immediately before the expected farrowing date. The reasoning is that a full gut takes up space in the abdomen and may slow labour. The evidence is mixed. A more practical approach is to offer the normal ration but not force consumption: most sows reduce their own intake naturally as farrowing approaches.

content.text: A dry sow needs 8 to 12 litres of water per day. This increases to 20 litres or more during lactation. Ensure the farrowing pen has free-flowing water before the sow enters it. A sow that arrives at farrowing already dehydrated will have poor milk let-down in early lactation.
